text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 17 January 2023 

Dr Priya Nair 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
St Thomas’ Hospital, Westminster Bridge Rd, London SE1 7EH  

Dear Dr Priya Nair, 

Alex Turner, DOB: 22-09-1987, Hospital No: K842719 NHS No. 9935471208  
Flat 12, 45 Millstream Court, London, SW8 1QP  

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent seizures with recent escalation in frequency and clustering; semiology now includes episodes progressing to generalised convulsive activity, consistent with suspected focal to generalised tonic–clonic pattern, though classification remains uncertain. 

Aetiology: 

Not established. 

Other Medical Problems: 

Generalised anxiety symptoms under primary care monitoring; intermittent insomnia related to occupational stress; vitamin D insufficiency previously treated. 

Management Plan: 

1) Adjust Lamotrigine to 150 mg mane, 150 mg evening for two weeks, then 200 mg mane, 200 mg evening if tolerated; maintain single branded preparation.  
2) Initiate Levetiracetam 250 mg twice daily for one week, then increase to 500 mg twice daily; advise to report mood or behavioural changes.  
3) Arrange urgent ambulatory EEG with sleep capture and MRI brain with epilepsy protocol.  
4) Serum Lamotrigine level, U&E, LFT, TFT, vitamin D, and bone profile today; repeat Lamotrigine level in 4 weeks post-titration.  
5) Safety counselling reinforced: no driving; avoid lone bathing, heights, open flames; discuss workplace adjustments to reduce triggers.  
6) Review in 8 weeks or sooner if further generalised convulsions occur; provide 24-hour contact pathway for deterioration. 

Since his last visit three months ago, there has been a clear deterioration. He reports eight events over the past six weeks, with three episodes culminating in bilateral convulsive activity lasting approximately 90–120 seconds, each followed by tongue biting, post-ictal confusion for up to 45 minutes, and myalgia. His partner describes preceding behavioural arrest and right-hand fumbling in two instances, though not in all. The events have clustered on workdays, particularly Monday to Wednesday, which he links to extended hours, early starts, and missed meals during periods of high workload. He denies alcohol excess and reports adherence to his current Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily regimen, brand consistent. There have been two near-falls at home; no head injury. No clear intercurrent illness. Sleep has been reduced to 5–6 hours on average. 

We discussed precipitating factors in detail. He will implement regular meal breaks, optimise sleep hygiene, and liaise with his employer regarding temporary adjustments to shift timing and workload intensity. I have provided a supporting statement for occupational health to mitigate known triggers and reduce risk, given the recent escalation and weekday clustering. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He reports eight events over the past six weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides a clear numeric statement of seizure events: "eight events over the past six weeks." This is the only explicit quantitative frequency. Subsets (three generalised convulsions) are part of the total and do not add separate events. There is mention of clustering but no quantifiable cluster count or size, so the cluster format is not applicable. Therefore, normalize the frequency directly as 8 per 6 weeks. To comply with the allowed format and unit rules, present as "8 per 6 week" (unit in singular).
seizure_frequency_number: He reports eight events over the past six weeks
reference: Seizure events suspected focal to bilateral tonic-clonic

flaws.description: Gold (8 per 6 week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
